speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor, Eva Kimel, maiden name, Najnudel, interviewer, Naomi Rappaport, city, Teaneck, state, New Jersey, country, USA, language, English. My name is Naomi Rappaport. And today is January 23, 1996. I am conducting an interview with Eva Najnudel Kimel in Teaneck, New Jersey, USA. Are you comfortable?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'm fine. At any time, I can take a drink?

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Mrs. Kimel. And thank you very much for welcoming us to your home and telling us your story, and sharing your experiences with the Shoah Foundation. I'd like to start with your name, please. And could you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Eva Kimel-- E-V-A in English. Was E-W-A in Polish. And second name is Kimel-- K-I-M-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was Najnudel, which is N-A-J-N-U-D-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your birth date, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: I usually don't like to speak about it, but my birthday is June 19, 1931.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll be 65 this year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born? In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-A-D-O-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Radom? What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Radom is in central Poland, near Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. I'd like to start your story with your early life and your memories of your early life, your home, where you lived. How large a city was Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Radom was a fairly large city. It was about 60,000 inhabitants, if I can remember correctly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your family always from Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's family was always from Radom. My father came from a little town, a small town, [? Ostrowsa-- ?] Ostrowiec, I'm sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was Ostrowiec in relation to Radom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ostrowiec was probably about an hour and a half or two hours from Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father owned owned a sawmill. And I think he acquired it with some partners after he got married to my mother. I understand that those days, parents of a girl had to give a dowry in order for the men to be able to develop some kind of a business or open a business. And I just heard about it recently, that there were some problems there. My father fell in love with my mother immediately when he saw her. But because of the money that my grandparents didn't want to give, there was slight problem. But then, finally, they settled. They settled. And they got married, and lived, for a few years, together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your-- the sawmill make any particular product?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they made parquet floors. And the sawmill was more to the east of Radom, was not-- it was more to, more to the east. It was very close to the Russian border. So my father really was spending all day-- a lot of time out there. And he was only a guest in our house, as far as I can remember. He was only coming home maybe for Saturday and then back. During the summer, for the-- we used to go there and spend the time with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the, the sawmill? It was in Radom or another city?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the sawmill was in Zawada-- S-- Z-A-W-A-D-A, near [INAUDIBLE], which was a bigger, bigger town. Because Zawada was really like a village in the middle of a forest. Because they used to buy the woods, the trees, and you know, process them into parquet floors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far is that, did you say, from Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was-- that must have been about seven hours, eight hours by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went there for the summers?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. What do you remember of your home? What did it look like? What kind of house did you live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a, I think, a three-- three story building. It was an apartment building that was really owned by my father's sister. And we lived on the third floor. We had the nice two bedroom, as we would call it here, apartment, with a large kitchen, I remember, and small bathroom. And it was, was a nice apartment for those, you know, for those days, those standards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a modern apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was a modern apartment. [? You could see. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: You had electricity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Radom was a large enough city to have all these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the area in which you lived? Was it a, a residential area, a business area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, in, in the Polish cities those days, there was no big differentiation because-- between the residential and commercial, like we know it here in the United States. Everything was really mixed in. So you could have had stores underneath and businesses. And like my uncle had business in his apartment on the second floor. That's the uncle that owned the building. He had a business right then and there. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had leather business. So it really was mixed, but was mostly residential, I would say, because they were, you know, quite nice apartment buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: My school was not too far away from, from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a private school, a private, Jewish school. So I learned, I remember, I learned about the holidays. And I learned, I guess, a little Hebrew, but can't remember from those days at all. But I remember learning about Passover and about asking the four questions. So those things, I do remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family an observant family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my family was observant. They were not ultra-religious. But my, my mother did have a kosher home. And my grandparents had a kosher home. And I know that every Saturday, I remember that I was, I was asked to come to dinner to my grandparents. And even if my mother, my parents didn't go, an uncle always came and picked me up and took me there. I was the first grandchild. So I was a big [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your mother's parents, or your father's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was my mother's parents. Now my father's mother was deceased. I never remembered her. And her name-- I was named after her. My grandfather, my father's father, was ultra-orthodox. So as we will see on the picture that later on, he wore a [? capata ?], and he wore-- he had a beard. And, and it's very interesting, which I didn't remember and my mother was telling me, that he only ate-- when he came to visit us from Ostrowiec to Radom, he only stayed with us, and only ate in my mother's house. Because my mother was more kosher, he thought, than his other daughters that lived in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your other grandparents, what language did they speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, with me, they spoke Polish. Amongst themselves, I would say they spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in your house, what did they speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my house, they spoke Polish. But when they didn't want me to understand, then they spoke Yiddish. So naturally, I learned how to understand. Because I wanted to know what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my school, the language was Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say you went to a private Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a politically private school? In other words, did it have a political bent of any sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was religion taught in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had to be. Because I remember, I was learning about the holidays and about, about Jewishness. So I guess it had to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a coed school, girls and boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. It might have been. Yeah, it probably was.

speaker: interviewer
text: To get back to friends and school, did you play with non-Jewish children, with Polish children at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, because, I guess, we lived in, in a different world, where we went to school with the same type of children. And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I don't think that my mother let me, let me down to go downstairs and play with everybody. Because you know, I was always either with my mother, or with the maid, or with somebody. So I don't remember if I was playing, you know, with non-Jewish children. I mean, I remember my very good friends were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe a typical day, what you did on a typical day in Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as far as my memory goes, I don't remember time we got up, but we had breakfast. And after that, at 10 o'clock every morning, I went downstairs to my aunt, who owned the building. And there, I had my second breakfast. Because apparently, I was a little chubby girl. And I loved to eat. And after that, we would-- my would probably-- I would go back upstairs. And we would go to a cafe house with my mother. And we would have another piece of danish, because I was not allowed to have any other piece of cake because it wasn't good for me-- piece of danish. My mother would spend, I guess, an hour, two hours there. And then we would, I guess, go back home. But this was the days when I didn't have school. Well, when I had school, then I guess I would be taken, walked to school. After school, I guess I would come home. They would bring me back home for dinner. Because dinner was at-- main meal was at 2 o'clock. And then I had to do homework. And I guess, again, I was walked to a friend's house. Or a friend would come to my house. And we would play. Or they would take me to the park, I guess, and for a walk, or to play in the park.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents have any relationship with the Polish population in Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother-- my father was in Radom really very little, because his business was really in Zawada. So he spent most of the time there. My mother, I doubt it. Because she, again, had a large family. And she was very close with, you know, with her parents. And her parents had a fur, fur business. So if you had nothing to do, you would go in to the-- to visit your parents and spend a little time there. My father probably, from the business point of view, probably did have to deal with the Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you considered a wealthy family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Upper, upper middle class, I would say, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you familiar at all with the [? Cahilla ?] structure in Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was a little girl, and I-- All I know that my two cousins emigrated to Israel. And I know that they were Zionists. And my father helped them financially to, to leave Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd just like to get back a little bit to your home life. How were holidays celebrated? And where were they celebrated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were either celebrated in our house, or most of the holidays were really celebrated in my grandparents' house. My grandparents had a big family. There were eight children, of which only one was abroad, was in France, studying medicine. Everybody else was home. So when we sat down around the table, there were quite a few of us. And I remember the holidays, either Friday nights, or Saturday dinners, or other holidays. It's interesting, because I still remember Passover being celebrated in our house. And remember that I was the youngest one, so I asked the four questions. And I opened the doors for the [? Elijah, ?] [? Elijah ?] to come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of what was going on in, in Germany and in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not then. Towards the end of '38-- and I remember vividly. We were in the Zawada at that time. That was the summer of '38. And all of a sudden, I heard people coming and going, and a lot of commotion, talking that a war might break out and whatever. But somehow, nobody believed in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you were a little girl at the time. Did you-- were you aware of people coming, Jews coming from Germany who were born in Poland and being thrown out of Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- was the word Hitler, or the name Hitler ever mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with, you know, with talking about the war, that the Germany might strike. And there might be a war. Yes, Hitler's name was, was mentioned. And I remember there was Czechoslovakia that he, that he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Annexed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Declared war, war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any attempt on anyone in your family, other than these two cousins, to go to Palestine, to leave Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- and that, I didn't learn then, I learned much later on-- that my father's sister was already at the train station. And she was going to leave. And I don't remember who that was, but started crying tremendously, somebody from the family, that she's leaving, and nobody will see her, et cetera. And she-- at last minute, she changed her mind, so remained in Poland, and was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she going?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was going to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, excuse me. She was going to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was going to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when the war broke out in '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Poland was invaded.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I remember-- I don't remember the minute when it broke out. But I remember, like, a day or two days later, my father came and said, let's leave. We are going to Zawada. So though my mother wasn't so crazy about it because she didn't want to leave her family, he rented a truck and packed us up. My grand-- my grandparents gave my mother a big-- not case. What did they call it? Not suitcase, but a big--

speaker: interviewer
text: A trunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trunk-- a big trunk of fur she should take with her. So we loaded everything on the, on the truck. And we took a cousin with us, who was a daughter of my aunt from the building. And she left with us. And I think it was on the 3rd or the 5th of September, 1939 where we left. And we were, I think, ones of the last ones to leave before Radom was shelled and bombed. And, and that's how our journey started.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- how long did it take you to get to Zawada?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took us a few days, because we had to stop on the way. And you know, we couldn't go because there were bombings already. And there were military soldiers. And you know, they, they were not letting the private vehicles through. So it took us quite a few days. I don't remember exactly how long.

speaker: interviewer
text: You drove in a truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who drove it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had a chauffeur.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a chauffeur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were on top of the truck. It was like an open dump truck, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take furniture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We just took the, we just took the personal, probably, personal belongings. And the fur, I remember, because my mother was talking about it later on. And we lived. You know, we lived on it, too. Because we were-- when we didn't have other means, whatever, my mother was selling it. And, and that was our income.

speaker: interviewer
text: En route, you say you-- there were military stoppings and bombings. Did you encounter any Germans at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, probably not yet. Not yet, because we were going east. And the Germans were coming from the west. Also, going back, what I remember when the war started that, you know, when there were raids, the windows had to be all taped up so there were not-- the glass was not shattering. And so that, that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father subject to military--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Induction?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the course of this trip--

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did anything of any event happen to you? What kind of areas did you pass, go through?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember. All I remember, we got to Zawada. And we stayed there, maybe a few days, or-- and the Russians were there already, in Zawada. And my father went to the train station, I guess to see what's going on, and to, to feel, feel the situation, and came home and told my mother, let's go to Russia. Because he spoke-- he met some Russians there. And they told him we can get on the train and, and, and go to Russia to live. But my mother would never do that. Because she didn't want to leave her family behind. And she said, no way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there. And from there, we started-- I guess we had to leave, because-- I don't remember whether that was-- which year that was. But that had to be '39, probably. We started moving east. Did the Germans ever get to Zawada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they did. But we were not there anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave Zawada?

speaker: interviewee
text: We must have stayed maybe a week to two weeks, not-- as far as my recollection, you know? I don't think it was more than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Germans were, you know, moving, moving east. And we had to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of the various laws and orders against Jews that the Germans had imposed in other parts of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not yet. Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not yet. Not yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was too early yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so why did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is possible that my, my father heard of, you know, some things. And we knew that we would be safer under the, under the Russians than under the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to his sawmill when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. He just left it.

speaker: interviewer
text: He--

speaker: interviewee
text: He just left it. It Is possible that people were started telling him, you better leave. Because you know, because you might be killed. Everybody was running away. And I guess everybody's feeling was that, that the people-- the Jews were safer under the Russians than under the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is Zawada a large community?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Zawada was a little village, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there many Jews in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Not at all. Maybe the few Jews that were there were the people that were-- that my father was in business with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from Zawada?

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd been to quite a few towns. The, the one of that stands out in my mind the most was Luck-- L-U-C-K. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and in Luck, we spent, probably-- we had to spend quite a long time. Because I remember I went to school in Luck. And in Luck, there were Russians already.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large is Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. But it was a middle-- it was a little town, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in an apartment, but I, I really-- well, I-- it was, I think, a one-story, a one-story building. And there was some Russian people that rented the apartment to us. And I, I went to school there. And there already, I had Ukrainian friends, and Polish friends, and all kinds of friends. I remember that behind our apartment there was-- it was a hilly town, I guess. It had mountains or whatever. And we used to sleigh ride. And, you know, for so a year or so, we had a normal life. My father was working there. Because, I guess he was experienced in the, in the mill. So he did get a job. And, and an uncle, one of my uncles, ran away from, from Radom, and somehow caught up with us. And he was staying-- he was with us, too. And, and this was-- and I don't remember. It might have been 1940. That was still before the, the outbreak of the Russian-German war. And one day, somebody-- the Russian people, the Ukranian people that we lived with-- tipped my parents off that the Russians are coming, and that they are taking all the refugees and sending them to Siberia. And my parents-- now, this I was told. I don't remember this incident. But they jumped out the windows. And they hid in the back of the building in a, like-- what do you call it, where the horses-- in a stable. And they left the two of us. Me and my sister, they left home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Russians really came in and asked for my parents. The people said they didn't know where they were. So they wanted to take me and my sister. And the people stood up. And they said, over their dead bodies, which was really very brave of them. They didn't know the-- They were Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. They didn't know the Russians at that time yet. Because they could have been shot right then and there. But they told them they were not going to give the children away. They should come back and, and take the whole family. But she, she wasn't giving the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they, they left. And after that-- my cousin, also, that we took with us from Radom was with us up to that point. Now, she told me later on that, from then on, she left. Because she came back. And she didn't find us. So apparently, right after my, my parents came back, they found out about it. We had to pack and leave. Because they were afraid that my parents were going to be-- that we are going to be taken, which maybe would have been better. Maybe my father would have lived if they took us to Siberia. But who know? From there, we went to a little town named Ludwipol. It's spelled L-U-D-W-I-P-O-L. And was it east? West?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was east.

speaker: interviewer
text: East.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was east. We were going east.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everything was east.

speaker: interviewee
text: We might have been in some little towns all along the way. But I really cannot recall. I remember that I used to be in orphanages-- not, maybe not orphanages, but in like daycare center. And I remember laying on a cot in the afternoon and sleeping. And I said, well, I'm going to write a book one day, if I survive. But everything just-- I, I, I can't recall these days. I mean, I just remember very slight moments and whatever, but not the whole story. Anyhow, we went to Ludwipol, Poland. Again, it was a little town. My father got a job there. My uncle caught up with us-- another uncle caught up with us, my mother's, well, still younger brother. My mother was the oldest. And he was really the-- he was in, in the fur business with my grandfather. So he started really selling business, and do some selling and going from town to town and trying to make some living. My father got a job there, again. And I went to school there, again. So I really had, like, two years of Russian schooling. And I remember, it was my birthday on the 19th of June. And my mother made me a big birthday party. And I had a lot of Ukrainian friends, some Polish friends, and whatever. Because by then, life had changed. And the next day, I woke up. And everybody started saying the war, the war broke out 20th of June, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going to change it our tape now.
